title: After 6 years trading gold manually I decided to build my own automated strategy.
author: Loose-Object-8913
selftext: Short Video Of a Test I Did.

comments.author: deepthinklabs_ai
comments.body: What type of result yah seeing so far? This is cool 👍

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body: I am glad you like it! So far with the Gold market reacting to the war, the EA is stepping a side. This week no trades Placed. The EA has Buy blocks once that Buy block is broken then Sell block is active. So EA will say No H4 Regime. Simple terms the EA saying this market is ranging.

comments.author: Loose-Object-8913
comments.body:
A few people asked how the system works so here is the basic structure:

• H4 trend filter using EMA slope
• H1 execution entries
• Pullback entries during trend continuation
• Breakout entries during strong momentum
• ATR based risk management
• Optional pyramiding during strong trends

Primary market is XAUUSD but I’ve been experimenting with indices as well.

Still refining things but the goal is a simple trend continuation system rather than over-optimised strategies.

replies.author: Clear_Ad6637
replies.body:
How do you define a pullback in a rule-based system? Is it a simple EMA breakout in H1 timeframe in the same direction of the H4 trend?  
Do you use MACD or RSI to define "strong momentum"?

This system looks simple and effective, I like it!

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body:
Hey, thanks for the kind words man, really appreciate it!

The pullback in this EA is actually not a simple EMA breakout on H1 in the direction of the H4 trend. Its a proper multi-timeframe rule. First the H4 has to be in a confirmed bullish regime based on the EMA150 plus upward slope over the last few bars, plus ADX strength and DI alignment. Then the previous H4 candle must have pulled back and touched or come really close to that H4 EMA within about 0.5 ATR while still closing above it. After that the actual entry happens on H1 where the previous candle pulls back and touches the H1 EMA50 within 1 ATR but closes above it with a decent body size and a few extra quality checks.

The breakout entries are a totally separate optional mode that triggers on a strong H4 close above the 10-bar high with a big body, not the main pullback logic.

For strong momentum we dont use MACD at all. We rely on ADX plus DI direction in the H4 regime instead, and RSI is just a simple overbought filter to stay out of extremes. Thats what keeps the system riding real trends without getting chopped up.

Happy to answer any other questions or explain more if you want, just let me know!

comments.author: Brilliant-Worth-3220
comments.body: Just do machine learning, this is the future of trading, the best machine will print money, collect data right now

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body:
Machine learning is definitely interesting, especially for pattern detection and signal generation.

For this project though I focused more on translating my manual trading logic into clear rule-based execution. That made it easier to test and understand how the system behaves in different market conditions.

Down the road I’d be interested in experimenting with ML as an additional signal layer, but for now I wanted something transparent and easy to validate.

replies.author: Brilliant-Worth-3220
replies.body: its a good approach, as i said in a previous post i think humans are better in adaptability, combined to long readingand comprehension data of ai we can do great things, so data isnt nothing without adaptability, good luck !

replies.author: Clear_Ad6637
replies.body:
What do you think is the best way to apply machine learning?   
I made a symple bollinger bands bot to get a lot of data of entries, and then labeled them, 1 is TP and 0 SL. After that I made another bot that used this XGB model to predict if an entry was TP or SL, and made a filter like prediction > 0.8 to filter low probability trades. It increased the winrate a little bit, but that's all.

I made just for playing with ML, not real trading. The main problem that I detected was the lack of data, to get a lot of entries I had to trade in lower timeframes, that are noisier, and didn't like it so much :(

replies.author: Brilliant-Worth-3220
replies.body:
Add another variable like volume, why? Because Bollinger Bands are directly linked to volatility and volume often confirms or invalidates price movements. It gives your model extra context to distinguish real breakouts from fake ones.

Speak in percentage for price variations rather than absolute values. It makes your data comparable across different assets and timeframes, and helps the model generalize instead of anchoring on specific price levels.

And sort your data by success percentage, what I mean by that is pre-filtering your data to reduce noise. Group your trades by feature combinations (like volume level , band position, price variation pourcentage), calculate the winrate of each group, and only keep the groups that show a statistically significant edge. That way you feed cleaner data to your model instead of letting it learn from pure noise.

Just be careful and make sure each group has enough trades to be meaningful, and validate on out-of-sample data to avoid overfitting on patterns that only worked by luck.

That said, keep in mind that in lower timeframes the market is very noisy and trade outcomes tend towards 50/50. If your base strategy is essentially a coin flip, no ML model will turn it into a winning machine, it'll just overfit on noise.

replies.author: algoholic20
replies.body: I am just starting to learn algo trading with machine learning. I only know how to make algo on mql5. What programming language would you recommend for algo trading with ML

comments.author: The_Shadow_Tiger
comments.body:
Hi, thanks for sharing!

I'm also trying to build my own EA for MT5 with the help of Claude and ChatGPT however as a newbie in this area I'm a bit los in what tools best to use and what are the recommended steps, so perhaps I will DM you for some guidance if you don't mind :)

Also wanted to ask you if this bot you built would be suitable for Prop firm?

Thank you!

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body:
Hey mate, thanks for the kind words and for reaching out!

On the prop firm question ,yeah, this EA is actually built with prop challenges in mind. It uses super conservative 0.5% risk per trade by default, ATR-based stops, and has that equity protection feature that automatically pauses everything if drawdown hits 12% (you can tweak it lower if your firm is stricter like 5-8%). No martingale, no crazy grid, just clean trend continuation with pyramiding only when already in profit and spaced by ATR. Most firms like FTMO, MyForexFunds, The5ers or FundedNext are fine with it on XAUUSD as long as you pass their consistency rules and don't trade during high-impact news if they ban it. I always recommend running it first on their demo challenge account for a couple weeks to be 100% sure it fits their exact rules. Make any adjustments to inputs to stay within the prop firm rules (some can be very strict)

As for building your own EA, no worries at all , everyone starts somewhere!

MetaEditor comes with MT5 best one to use in my opinion. 

 Recommended steps I usually tell newbies: start with a super simple strategy on paper (like one moving average cross), code it piece by piece in MQL5, test on history data, forward test on demo for at least a month, then go live small. If you want I can help you correct or improve any code you have – feel free to DM me anytime with snippets, happy to take a look and sort it out.

Let me know how the challenge goes or if you have more questions!

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body: Also want to add that I am currently working on a FTMO preset following their rules. Will hopefully have that up in the next week or so.
